eras.name: Roman Antiquity
eras.description: Fashion was a indicator of social status and citizenship, characterized by draped wool and linen garments. The silhouettes emphasized hierarchy through color, specifically the use of Tyrian purple.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Historical illustration of a Roman Senator in a white wool toga with a purple border and a noblewoman in a layered linen stola, ancient Roman forum background, cinematic lighting, hyper-realistic textures.

eras.name: The High Renaissance
eras.description: A period of opulent display centered in Florence and Venice, featuring heavy brocades, velvets, and intricate embroidery. Menswear introduced the doublet while women wore high-waisted gowns with voluminous slashed sleeves.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 16th-century Florentine noble wearing a rich velvet doublet with puffed slashed sleeves and gold embroidery, oil painting style, chiaroscuro lighting, Renaissance palace interior.

eras.name: The Risorgimento & Unification
eras.description: Italian fashion aligned with broader European Romanticism but maintained a distinct focus on fine tailoring and regional silk production. The silhouette transitioned from Empire waistlines to the structured Victorian crinoline.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 19th-century Italian aristocrats in Milan, man in a tailored black frock coat, woman in a wide silk crinoline dress with lace detailing, neoclassical architecture background.

eras.name: The Birth of 'Made in Italy'
eras.description: The 1951 Sala Bianca show in Florence launched Italian high fashion globally. This era focused on 'La Dolce Vita' aesthetics, blending Hollywood glamour with impeccable Mediterranean craftsmanship.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 1950s Italian chic, a woman in a fitted floral cocktail dress and cat-eye sunglasses sitting at a Roman cafe, Vespa in the background, vintage film photography style.

eras.name: The Rise of the Milanese Stylists
eras.description: Milan replaced Florence as the fashion capital as ready-to-wear exploded. Giorgio Armani redefined the professional wardrobe with unstructured jackets, while Versace introduced provocative, high-octane glamour.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 1980s Milanese fashion, a model in an oversized greige Armani power suit with soft shoulders, minimalist urban background, high-contrast fashion editorial photography.

eras.name: The New Era of Luxury
eras.description: A shift toward conceptual luxury and heritage preservation. This era is defined by Prada's 'ugly-chic' philosophy and a renewed focus on sustainable artisanal production within the 'Made in Italy' framework.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Contemporary Italian street style in Milan, high-end technical nylon outerwear mixed with heritage wool trousers, sleek modern architecture, sharp focus, 8k resolution.

textiles.name: Como Silk
textiles.description: World-renowned silk produced in the Lake Como region, known for its incredible luster and precision printing.
textiles.technique: Screen and digital printing on high-density silk weave

textiles.name: Biella Wool
textiles.description: Superfine merino wool and cashmere fabrics used by the world's top tailors for luxury suiting.
textiles.technique: Traditional worsted spinning and weaving using Alpine water for finishing

textiles.name: Florentine Leather
textiles.description: Supple, vegetable-tanned leather used for high-end accessories and footwear.
textiles.technique: Traditional vegetable tanning using plant-based tannins

textiles.name: Venetian Velvet
textiles.description: Heavy, plush fabric often featuring 'soprarizzo' techniques with raised patterns.

climate.fashionImplication: The climate encourages 'Sprezzatura' or effortless layering; lightweight linens and fresce wools are essential for hot summers, while high-quality wool overcoats define the damp, cool winters.

culturalInfluences: The Concept of 'Bella Figura' (Presenting one's best self)

notableEvents: Met Gala: Heavenly Bodies (Italian influence focus)
